title: Decoding Crystallographic Surface Chirality with Machine Learning: From Atomic Geometry to Fermi Surface Projections
authors: Chetana Badala Viswanatha, Ka Man Yu, Benito Arnoldi, Anagha Aravind, Aaruni Kaushik, Jannis Lessmeister, Martin Aeschlimann, Benjamin Stadtmüller, S. Harshini Tekur
abstract: Intrinsically chiral metal surfaces, where handedness arises from the asymmetric step-kink-terrace topology of high-Miller-index planes, are model systems for enantiospecific catalysis, sensing, and spintronics. Yet, no consistent method exists to classify their handedness directly from experimental observables. We report a dual-domain machine learning framework that decodes crystallographic surface chirality from two independent image representations: atomic structure models in real space and simulated momentum-resolved photoemission maps of the Fermi surface projections in reciprocal space. ResNet18, a deep convolutional neural network, fine-tuned on a database of labeled images achieves ~73% classification accuracy on atomic models and ~99% on Fermi surface projections. We show that the latter transfers directly to synchrotron-acquired experimental images after fine-tuning on just two labeled frames. We identify a working correspondence between the two representations: just as the kink site geometry fixes the orientation of crystallographic planes in real space, the surface normal position in a momentum-resolved photoemission map anchors the orientation of the Fermi surface polygons in reciprocal space. It is precisely this relative orientation that encodes handedness into the map topology with high accuracy. The pronounced difference in accuracy shows that handedness is more readily recovered from the momentum-space electronic pattern than from the local atomic geometry of the kinked surface. This finding has direct implications for the disorder resilience of geometric chiral-induced spin selectivity (CISS) at realistic metal surfaces.
summary: This work introduces a machine learning framework to determine the handedness of chiral metal surfaces by analyzing two types of images: real-space atomic models and reciprocal-space Fermi surface maps. The authors found that classifying chirality from ARPES data is significantly more accurate than using local atomic geometry, suggesting that global electronic patterns are superior diagnostics for intrinsic material properties.
main_problem: The core problem is developing a reliable method to classify the handedness (chirality) of intrinsically chiral metal surfaces from experimental observables.
main_result: A dual-domain machine learning framework successfully decodes surface chirality, showing that electronic structure maps (ARPES/momentum space) provide significantly more robust classification ($\sim 99\%$) than local atomic geometry models ($\sim 73\%$).
method: The authors employed a deep convolutional neural network (ResNet18) trained on two distinct image representations: simulated real-space atomic structures and momentum-resolved photoemission maps from the Fermi surface.
model_or_system: The physical system involves high-Miller-index planes of FCC metals exhibiting geometric chirality due to asymmetric step-kink-terrace topology. The analysis is performed using both atomic structure models (real space) and simulated/experimental Angle-Resolved Photoemission Spectroscopy (ARPES) maps (reciprocal space).
key_observables: Chirality label ($	ext{R}$ or $	ext{S}$), classification accuracy, Fermi surface polygon orientation relative to the surface normal.
important_parameters: Photon energy ($21.2\ 	ext{eV}$ in simulations; $30\ 	ext{eV}$ experimentally); Classification metrics (Accuracy, AUC-ROC).
main_assumptions: The primary assumption is that chirality is encoded differently—locally vs. globally—in the two representations, and that ML can learn this topological invariant from image data.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate representative R/S atomic models alongside their corresponding simulated ARPES Fermi surface projections, demonstrating how mirror symmetry breaking manifests in both domains for classification training.
paper_structure: The paper follows a systematic pipeline: defining the physical problem (chirality detection), generating synthetic data for both real-space and reciprocal-space representations, training separate ResNet18 classifiers on each domain, and finally testing the superior performance of the reciprocal-space classifier on challenging experimental ARPES images.
why_interesting: The application of deep learning to extract fundamental topological invariants (chirality) from complex electronic band structure maps is highly relevant. The finding that momentum space provides a more robust signature than local atomic geometry has implications for understanding disorder resilience in quantum phenomena like CISS.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Representative images from the two datasets. (a, b) Atomic models of an R and S fcc kink surface. The three microfacet types at the kink site are indicated by coloured labels: (111) in blue (priority 1), (100) in green (priority 2), and (110) in red (priority 3). Tracing the sequence 1→2→3 proceeds clockwise for (a) the R surface and anticlockwise for (b) the S surface. (c, d) Corresponding simulated ARPES Fermi surface projections for the same Miller index pair (simulated for hν = 21.2 eV). White numerals 1, 2, and 3 mark the Fermi surface features associated with the (111), (100), and (110) microfacets, respectively. The mirror-symmetry breaking between (c) and (d) is evident in...

Figure 2: Test-set performance of the binary chirality classifier. (a) Normalized confusion matrix for classification of R and S enantiomers. (b) Held-out test-set metrics for the Fermi surface classifier
Figure 3: Experimentally acquired Fermi surface maps for the (a) R and (b) S surfaces of Cu(643), respectively (hν = 30 eV). Each panel is annotated with the classifier-predicted label.

Figure 4: Transfer-learning strategy for R/S chirality classification. Workflow for (a) train- ing on synthetic real-space and reciprocal-space (ARPES) images, and (b) simulation-to- experimental adaptation of the pretrained classifier.
